topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office smelled faintly of lavender and old paper, a stark contrast to the rough-and-tumble playground I remembered. I watched Dr. Elias Thorne across the reception desk. He was impeccably dressed, his movements slow and deliberate as he adjusted his spectacles. It was jarring. Elias, the boy who used to dismantle my lunch money with a smirk and a shove, now sat behind a mahogany desk, radiating a calm that felt entirely manufactured. Every time he offered a gentle, measured response to a client's distress, a knot tightened in my stomach. I found myself lingering near the doorway, my gaze fixed on the precise way his fingers tapped the notepad—a rhythm that seemed too controlled, too practiced. I kept my hands jammed deep into my pockets, a low thrum of unease vibrating beneath my skin.

[story 2]

"So, tell me more about the trauma," Dr. Thorne murmured, his voice a low, resonant balm. I sat on the edge of the velvet armchair, my knuckles white where I gripped my hands together. I tried to focus on the narrative he was weaving, but my attention kept snagging on the familiar set of his jaw, the way his eyes, usually narrowed in challenge back in the fifth grade, were now soft and empathetic. I shifted in my seat, the fabric of the chair scratching against my legs. A restless energy began to bubble up inside me, a need to test the boundaries of this strange new dynamic. I leaned forward, my tone deliberately challenging, trying to see if the old spark—the antagonism—could still ignite in his eyes.

[story 3]

I observed him from the periphery. Elias had transitioned from a loud, abrasive presence to this quiet architect of comfort. The way he listened, truly listened, made my jaw clench. It was an unsettling shift; the sharp edges I’d once known were sanded down, replaced by something smooth and unnervingly accommodating. When he offered a reassuring nod, a flicker of something sharp and defensive sparked deep within my chest. I stood by the window, pretending to examine the city below, but my focus was entirely on him. I noted the subtle, almost imperceptible pause before he spoke, a hesitation that felt less like thoughtfulness and more like calculation. I took a slow, shallow breath, trying to steady the quickening pulse in my neck.
